mission.title: Install and Configure Elastic Cloud on Kubernetes (ECK)
mission.description: Elastic Cloud on Kubernetes (ECK) automates the deployment and management of the Elastic Stack on Kubernetes. Use it when you need to run Elasticsearch, Kibana, and other Elastic products in a Kubernetes environment. Key trade-offs include the complexity of managing an operator and the need for Kubernetes expertise.

steps.title: Add the ECK Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the Elastic Helm repository to your Helm configuration, allowing you to install ECK from it.

```bash

helm repo add elastic https://helm.elastic.co

helm repo update

```

> This adds the official Elastic Helm repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install the ECK operator
steps.description:
This step installs the ECK operator in the 'elastic-system' namespace using Helm.

```bash

kubectl create namespace elastic-system

helm install eck-operator elastic/eck-operator --namespace elastic-system --version 2.14.0

```

> Create the namespace for ECK.
> Installs the ECK operator with a specific version.

steps.title: Verify the ECK operator installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the ECK operator is running correctly in the cluster.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n elastic-system

```

> Verify that the ECK operator pod is running.

steps.title: Deploy an Elasticsearch cluster
steps.description:
This step creates an Elasticsearch cluster using a custom resource definition (CRD).

```bash

cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: default
spec:
  version: 8.5.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.allow_mmap: false
EOF

```

> Creates an Elasticsearch cluster named 'my-elasticsearch'.
commands:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: default
spec:
  version: 8.5.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.allow_mmap: false
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Elasticsearch cluster
steps.description:
This step checks if the Elasticsearch cluster is running and ready.

```bash

kubectl get elasticsearch -n default

```

> Verify the status of the Elasticsearch cluster.

uninstall.title: Uninstall the ECK operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the ECK operator and all associated resources. Be aware that this will delete all Elasticsearch clusters managed by ECK, leading to data loss.

```bash

helm uninstall eck-operator --namespace elastic-system

kubectl delete namespace elastic-system

```

> Uninstalls the ECK operator.
> Deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the ECK operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you backup your Elasticsearch data. This step upgrades the ECK operator to a new version.

```bash

kubectl drain <node-name> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-local-data

helm upgrade eck-operator elastic/eck-operator --namespace elastic-system --version 2.14.1

kubectl uncordon <node-name>

```

> Drain the node to prepare for upgrade.
> Upgrades the ECK operator to the specified version.
> Uncordon the node after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to install CRD'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Custom Resource Definition (CRD) might not be installed correctly.

**Fix:**
Reinstall the CRDs using 'kubectl apply -f https://download.elastic.co/downloads/eck/2.14.0/crds.yaml'.

troubleshooting.title: Elasticsearch cluster status is 'red'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that one or more primary shards are not allocated.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the Elasticsearch pods for errors using 'kubectl logs <pod-name>'.

troubleshooting.title: Kibana is not accessible
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Kibana service might not be exposed correctly.

**Fix:**
Ensure the Kibana service is of type 'LoadBalancer' or 'NodePort'.

troubleshooting.title: ECK operator pod is in 'CrashLoopBackOff'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The ECK operator might be misconfigured or missing permissions.

**Fix:**
Check the operator logs for more details using 'kubectl logs <eck-operator-pod-name> -n elastic-system'.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Elasticsearch 9.x and improved TLS certificate management.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 2.12 or 2.13, ensure your Elasticsearch configurations are compatible with 9.x.

resolution.summary: You should see the Elasticsearch cluster status as 'green' when everything is working: 'kubectl get elasticsearch -n default'.
codeSnippets:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: default
spec:
  version: 8.5.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.allow_mmap: false
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the required resources.
Ensure your Kubernetes cluster meets the minimum resource requirements.
